records.rationale: These are two distinct test cases covering different `ChainId` variants. The setup and assertion shape are the same, but the duplicated structure is small and intentional in test code to verify separate inputs and outcomes; it does not indicate an unhealthy repeated implementation pattern.

The fragments are structurally similar, but they assert different entries in the signal gate matrix: SpecAdr merge strictness versus CatalogSpec merge strictness. In tests, this small repetition is acceptable because each case documents a distinct expected behavior with a specific failure name.

These are two narrowly scoped test cases covering different enum variants (`ChainId::CatalogSpec` vs `ChainId::ImplCatalog`). The shared setup is minimal and the duplication is justified by distinct expected coverage rather than copy-pasted logic that should be abstracted.

These are two distinct matrix-cell tests, not a repeated implementation of the same behavior. The only shared structure is the standard test setup and assertion pattern; the differing `GateKind` values exercise different inputs and expected outcomes, so the duplication is intentional test coverage rather than a DRY problem.

These are two separate test cases covering different inputs and expected outcomes (`Commit` -> `Interim` vs `Merge` -> `Strict`). The shared setup is minimal and intentional for readability in a test matrix, so this is not a meaningful DRY violation.

These fragments are similar test scaffolds, but they validate different domain cases and expected outcomes: `ChainId::CatalogSpec` must resolve to `Strict`, while `ChainId::ImplCatalog` must resolve to `Interim`. Reusing the same setup pattern in separate tests is normal here and does not indicate harmful duplication.

These snippets are intentionally similar test cases, but they exercise different `ChainId` inputs and assert different enum values. The repetition is minimal and localized to a short unit test, so it does not create a meaningful DRY problem.

These are two separate test cases covering different `ChainId` variants (`SpecAdr` vs `AdrUser`) with the same shared setup and assertion shape. The repetition is limited to a small test pattern and serves distinct behavior coverage, so it is not a meaningful DRY violation.

The fragments repeat a very small test pattern, but they validate different entries in the signal gate matrix: `ChainId::AdrUser` versus `ChainId::ImplCatalog`. The duplication is localized, explicit, and in test code where separate named cases improve coverage readability without creating a shared maintenance hazard.

These are two small test cases that intentionally share the same setup/assertion structure but verify different inputs and outcomes (`Commit -> Interim` vs `Merge -> Strict`). The overlap is minimal and normal for unit tests, so this is not a meaningful DRY violation.

These fragments are intentionally similar test cases but they exercise different behavior: one asserts `SpecAdr` resolves `Strict` for `GateKind::Commit`, the other for `GateKind::Merge`. Repeating the setup in separate focused tests is appropriate here because each test documents a distinct rule and failure mode.

The fragments are two small, table-like unit tests that assert distinct matrix entries: SpecAdr merge strictness and ImplCatalog merge strictness. The repeated setup/assertion shape is intentional for per-chain coverage and keeps each expected gate behavior explicit; extracting this two-line pattern would not remove meaningful logic duplication.

These two fragments follow the same test pattern, but they exercise different inputs (`ChainId::SpecAdr` vs `ChainId::CatalogSpec`) and verify distinct cases. In test code, that level of repetition is usually acceptable because each assertion documents a separate behavior rather than duplicating implementation logic.

These fragments are two distinct test cases that exercise different inputs and expected outcomes (`ChainId::AdrUser` -> `Interim` versus `ChainId::SpecAdr` -> `Strict`). The shared setup is minimal and normal in table-style unit tests, so this does not indicate harmful duplication.

The two fragments cover different input combinations and expected outcomes for `ChainGateEntry::resolve`. The existing test verifies an interim commit gate with a strict merge gate, while the changed test verifies a strict commit gate with an interim merge gate. They exercise the same method, but they are not redundant copies of the same case.

The two fragments are similar test cases, but they exercise different input combinations and assertions. The repeated `ChainGateEntry` construction is a small, purposeful setup duplication in tests, not a harmful DRY violation.

These two fragments share the same test fixture setup, but they exercise different behavior: one verifies `resolve(GateKind::Merge)` and the other verifies `resolve(GateKind::Commit)`. Reusing the same literal values in nearby tests is normal and does not indicate a meaningful DRY problem here.

These are two distinct test cases that assert the same contract for different `ChainId`/`GateKind` combinations. The repeated setup is minimal and confined to tests, so this does not rise to a meaningful DRY violation.

These fragments show two different layer crates exposing similarly named modules (`dry_check`, `semantic_dup`, `spec`, `track`, etc.), but that is not DRY duplication by itself. The domain crate re-exports core types and ports, while the infrastructure crate exposes concrete adapters and codecs. The overlap is architectural naming alignment, not repeated implementation or duplicated logic.

The overlap here is structural, not duplicated implementation logic. `libs/domain/src/lib.rs` is a domain-layer barrel that re-exports domain types and modules, while `libs/usecase/src/lib.rs` is a separate use-case-layer barrel that re-exports selected domain identity types and declares its own use-case modules. The shared names reflect layer boundaries and composition, not copy-pasted behavior, so this does not constitute a DRY violation.
